perturbation.original_text: 1.3. "Distributor" means Siemens, its Affiliates, or any Third Party which has been granted distribution rights whose scope includes the Products and/or Services by Siemens.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3. "Distributor" means Siemens exclusively and does not extend to its Affiliates or any Third Party, unless explicitly approved by Accuray in writing for each individual transaction. This approval must be secured prior to any distribution activities undertaken by the Affiliate or Third Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction to the definition of 'Distributor'. Originally, Siemens' Affiliates and Third Parties with distribution rights were included under the definition of 'Distributor'. This modification, however, limits 'Distributor' strictly to Siemens, requiring explicit Accuray approval for each transaction involving Affiliates or Third Parties. The contradiction is that elsewhere in the document, terms and conditions are applied to all Affiliates and distributors of Siemens, which may conflict with this narrower definition. For example section 3.13. Affiliates; Distributors. Siemens shall cause any of its Affiliates or distributors purchasing Systems or Services pursuant to the terms of this Agreement to agree to be bound by and comply with the terms and conditions of this Agreement and the provisions of the Strategic Alliance Agreement related to or applicable to such purchase, unless such Affiliate or distributor is already party to a distribution agreement for Products with Accuray.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.13. Affiliates; Distributors. Siemens shall cause any of its Affiliates or distributors purchasing Systems or Services pursuant to the terms of this Agreement to agree to be bound by and comply with the terms and conditions of this Agreement and the provisions of the Strategic Alliance Agreement related to or applicable to such purchase, unless such Affiliate or distributor is already party to a distribution agreement for Products with Accuray.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Distributor' to exclusively mean Siemens, requiring explicit Accuray approval for affiliates or third parties. This directly contradicts section 3.13, which holds Siemens responsible for ensuring that its Affiliates or distributors purchasing Systems or Services adhere to the agreement's terms. The contradiction arises because the original section 3.13 assumes Affiliates and Distributors are bound by the agreement without needing specific Accuray approval, while the modified definition requires such approval. This creates a strong in-text contradiction, leading to confusion and potential unenforceability regarding the obligations of Siemens' Affiliates and distributors. The change follows the definition for Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.4. Standard Lead Time. As of the Effective Time and to the best of Accuray's knowledge, Accuray's standard lead time for delivery of Products is six months.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.4. Standard Lead Time. Accuray aims to provide delivery of Products within a commercially reasonable timeframe, with no guarantee of a specific lead time. Any previously communicated lead times are purely estimates and non-binding.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces a specific 'standard lead time' (six months) with a vague commitment to a 'commercially reasonable timeframe'. This creates an in-text contradiction with section 4.1.2, which details how supplementary shipping costs arising from the need to meet the delivery deadline set forth in the Purchase Order by way of expedited delivery shall be borne by Accuray, if such delivery deadline was at least six months after the submission of such Purchase Order by Distributor. For example, if a Purchase Order was submitted on June 1, with a requested delivery date of December 1, any expedited delivery expenses required in order to ensure delivery by December 1 shall be borne by Accuray, while if the requested delivery date was October 1, any expedited delivery expenses required in order to ensure delivery by October 1 shall be borne by Distributor. The modified version removes a 'standard lead time' and with it the clarity of how shipping costs is determined. If no timeline is guaranteed who determines what is the 'reasonable time'
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1.2. Shipment. All shipments shall be made F.C.A. Port of Oakland, California, USA. Transfer of risk from Accuray to Distributor shall occur at such F.C.A. location as provided in F.C.A. terms and transfer of title shall occur at the same time. Distributor may request Accuray to use a particular freight carrier, and Accuray agrees to do so, if feasible. If not feasible in Accuray's reasonable judgment, then Accuray shall promptly advise Distributor of the reasons. If no such request is made, Accuray shall ship in accordance with any instructions contained in the Purchase Order or via FedEx ground, with no extra insurance. Accuray shall bill any actual freight costs to Distributor. Any supplementary shipping costs arising from the need to meet the delivery deadline set forth in the Purchase Order by way of expedited delivery shall be borne by Accuray, if such delivery deadline was at least six months after the submission of such Purchase Order by Distributor. For example, if a Purchase Order was submitted on June 1, with a requested delivery date of December 1, any expedited delivery expenses required in order to ensure delivery by December 1 shall be borne by Accuray, while if the requested delivery date was October 1, any expedited delivery expenses required in order to ensure delivery by October 1 shall be borne by Distributor.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a definite 'standard lead time' (six months) with a vague 'commercially reasonable timeframe', removing the guarantee of a specific lead time. This directly contradicts section 4.1.2, which states, 'Any supplementary shipping costs arising from the need to meet the delivery deadline set forth in the Purchase Order by way of expedited delivery shall be borne by Accuray, if such delivery deadline was at least six months after the submission of such Purchase Order by Distributor.' By removing the six-month benchmark, the responsibility for expedited shipping costs becomes unclear, creating an in-text contradiction and uncertainty about the obligations of each party.

perturbation.original_text: 4.6.1. Scope of Warranty. Accuray will provide a warranty to each Customer that the Products will be free from material defects and perform substantially in accordance with the written Specifications provided by Accuray as reflected in the regulatory clearance at the time of sale for a period of one (1) year following Installation of the Products at Customer's facility, but not to exceed eighteen (18) months following shipment of such Products to Distributor ("Warranty Period").
perturbation.changed_text: 4.6.1. Scope of Warranty. Accuray will provide a warranty to each Customer that the Products will be free from material defects, provided that such defects are reported within 30 days of discovery and verified by Accuray's internal testing procedures. The duration of the warranty is at Accuray's sole discretion and may vary based on product type and customer relationship.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction in the warranty terms. The original text clearly defines the 'Warranty Period' as one year following installation (up to 18 months from shipment). This modification introduces uncertainty about the warranty duration. The specific 30 days of discovery reported makes this statement contradictory in conjunction with the 1 year mentioned in the original text. This will make Section 4.6.2 Hardware and Software. If a Customer notifies Accuray in writing during the Warranty Period of a defect in a Product that causes the Product to fail to conform to the foregoing warranty, Accuray shall at its option either repair or replace the non- conforming Product or, if in Accuray's opinion such repair or replacement is not commercially reasonable, Accuray shall refund a pro-rated portion of the price paid by the Customer for such Product calculated based on a straight-line depreciation over a 5-year period beginning on the date of delivery. This will be Accuray's sole and exclusive obligation and such Customer's sole and exclusive remedy in relation to defective Products and parts, difficult to enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.6.2. Hardware and Software. If a Customer notifies Accuray in writing during the Warranty Period of a defect in a Product that causes the Product to fail to conform to the foregoing warranty, Accuray shall at its option either repair or replace the non- conforming Product or, if in Accuray's opinion such repair or replacement is not commercially reasonable, Accuray shall refund a pro-rated portion of the price paid by the Customer for such Product calculated based on a straight-line depreciation over a 5-year period beginning on the date of delivery. This will be Accuray's sole and exclusive obligation and such Customer's sole and exclusive remedy in relation to defective Products and parts.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an in-text contradiction because it replaces a defined 'Warranty Period' (one year following installation) with a warranty duration at Accuray's sole discretion, contingent on reporting defects within 30 days and verification by Accuray. This directly conflicts with section 4.6.2, which refers to actions taken 'during the Warranty Period.' The modified text makes the duration of the warranty uncertain, undermining the enforceability of 4.6.2, because the 'Warranty Period' is no longer well-defined, thus meeting the criteria for a strong in-text contradiction.
